data.title: National Games closing ceremony marks more of a beginning than end for Greater Bay Area
data.description:
The curtain came down on the National Games on Friday, wrapping up a two-week sporting celebration with a closing ceremony that felt much more like a beginning than the end of an occasion steeped in historic achievement.
After a gold, silver and bronze-fuelled build-up, this was the Greater Bay Area’s coming out party as a region ready to host the biggest of sporting events.
And in the new Happy Theatre, where water was its stage and the eponymous harbour in Shenzhen its spectacular backdrop, the ceremony had a venue fit for any opening or closing ceremony.
Given its location and history, the wet stuff played a significant part in the evening’s events, with 225 synchronised swimmers and dragon boat dancers producing a display four months in the making.
Throughout, technology and performing art went hand in hand, and in the era of robots and artificial intelligence crossed over to stunning effect, never more so than in the appearance of an AI-generated tree sprouting from the water.

In many ways, the ceremony was an audition for greater things to come, and that message flowed through the performances and speeches that came before.
As they had at the opening ceremony, the leaders of the three co-hosts – Guangdong, Hong Kong and Macau – had their say, this time in front of Wang Huning, chairman of the National Committee of the Chinese People’s Political Consultative Conference.
Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu, who later told the Hong Kong delegation how proud he was of their “historic achievement”, said the fortnight’s success belonged to “every athlete, coach, referee, staff member and volunteer” involved.
“While the Games are drawing to a close, their spirit endures indefinitely,” Lee added. “Let us transform this spirit of perseverance into motivation for building a stronger sporting nation.”
Speaking first, Meng Fanli, the governor of Guangdong, hailed a “glorious sporting event” that had shown the world what the region was capable of, while pointing to its growing influence within China.
“This has fully demonstrated the Chinese path to modernisation in the Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macau Greater Bay Area, showcasing a vibrant landscape and the high-spirited energy of the Chinese people in the new era,” he said.

Earlier, Ceci Lee Sze-wing had arrived in a BYD sports car, a nod not only to Shenzhen’s status as a tech hub and China’s booming auto manufacturing industry, but to the cyclist’s achievement as the first Hong Kong athlete to win three golds at a single National Games.
That earned her HK$2.25 million of the HK$11.475 million the Hong Kong Jockey Club’s incentive award scheme paid out to the city’s athletes for the 19 medals they won.
There were some 419 medal events for the elite athletes involved over the course of the Games, and in a press conference held by the organising committee on Friday, Hong Kong Chief Secretary Eric Chan Kwok-ki, its vice-president, suggested the cooperation involved between the hosts showcased “the infinite possibilities for the joint development” of the bay area.
No doubt the tacit support for an Olympics bid raised by former International Olympic Committee member Thomas Bach 10 days ago has been hanging over what has happened since.
Chan was certainly keen to push the government’s promotion and industrialisation of the sports industry, one where the 1,600 elite athletes the city has on its books are supported by HK$1 billion in investment in the 2025-26 financial year.
“Hong Kong athletes have achieved historic results in the 15th National Games, presenting a golden opportunity for Hong Kong’s sports development,” he said.
“The government will work together with all sectors to convert the success of the National Games into long-term momentum, further improving local sports standards and promoting a culture of ‘sports for all’.”
Not that the city will go it alone. The Games have shown the hosts to be greater than the sum of their parts, although the individual characteristics were all on display as the ceremony wound down.
An image of Victoria Harbour was projected across the night sky and the Ruins of Saint Paul’s dominated a celebration of Macau’s involvement, all part of a final instalment titled “The Greater Bay Area Symphony”.
The next movement of that piece is still to come, and all that remains to be seen is what shape that takes.
“This [Games] showcased the unique features of the region as a strategic pivot point for the new development paradigm, a demonstration zone for high-quality development, and a leader in Chinese modernisation,” said Gao Zhidan, director of the State General Administration of Sports of China.

data.title: Hong Kong launches probe after voters’ personal details posted on social media
data.description:
Hong Kong authorities have launched an investigation after the personal details of “some voters were uploaded to a social media platform” ahead of the Legislative Council election next month, with the government strongly condemning the act.
The government said that following the discovery on Friday, the Registration and Electoral Office had referred the case to a law enforcement agency, which was conducting a thorough investigation.
“The [Hong Kong] government strongly condemns the illegal act and solemnly reiterates that any attempt to disrupt or interfere with an election is a serious offence,” a spokesman said.
The government warned that such acts would not be tolerated and law enforcement action would be taken seriously. It did not elaborate on the voters involved.
It said the Office of the Privacy Commissioner for Personal Data had also initiated an investigation and immediately required the relevant social media platform to remove the information.
The platform had confirmed that it had complied and taken down the content, the government said.


A check by the Post found that social media platform Threads carried a post on Friday afternoon containing the personal information of several political figures, including their home addresses.
They included lawmakers Paul Tse Wai-chun, Regina Ip Lau Suk-yee and Starry Lee Wai-king. Lee is running for election in the Kowloon Central geographical constituency.
However, those posts had been deleted by Friday evening.
The Legco election will be held on December 7, with 161 candidates running for 90 seats.
Among the 161 hopefuls, 51 are competing in the geographical constituencies for 20 seats, while 60 are standing in the 28 functional constituencies for 30 seats. The remaining 50 candidates will contest the 40 seats in the Election Committee constituency.
Residents vote for their representatives for the geographical constituencies while lawmakers in the other two constituencies are chosen by a small circle of voters within their respective sectors.
Click here for the full list of candidates.

data.title: Swiss police hunt 2 men after gold heist at museum, in echoes of Louvre robbery
data.description:
Swiss authorities were searching on Friday for two robbers who overpowered a security guard at an ancient Rome-themed museum in Lausanne, smashed a display case and made off with dozens of gold coins that had been displayed inside.
City police said the suspects had bought tickets and waited until other visitors had left shortly before closing time on Thursday.
“The two individuals assaulted and overpowered the guard. They then broke into a secured display case and stole several gold coins that were displayed inside,” before fleeing, the police statement said.
The 64-year-old guard had activated the museum’s panic alarm, triggering a rapid response by law enforcement, police said.
The monetary value of the loot was not immediately revealed, but police said the coins had “archaeological value”.

“At this stage, an inventory is under way to determine the exact number of items stolen and to identify any other missing items,” the statement said.
The theft comes at a time when gold prices have soared in global markets this year – even if they have dropped off their highs lately – and a high-profile robbery at the Louvre in Paris exposed vulnerabilities and security lapses at museums.
Officials said the Lausanne museum employee, a Swiss national, was interviewed by investigators and that he was not injured in the incident. No other people – staff or visitors – were on hand at the time.
State prosecutors have opened an investigation. Lausanne city officials filed a legal complaint for damage to the museum, and the regional government – the owner of the gold coins – announced plans to file a criminal complaint.
Additional reporting by Agence France-Presse

data.title: Hard-right UK politician jailed for taking bribes to make pro-Russia statements
data.description:
A hard-right British politician and former ally of Reform UK leader Nigel Farage was sentenced Friday to 10½ years in prison for accepting bribes to make favourable statements about Russia in the European Parliament.
Nathan Gill, 52, pleaded guilty earlier this year to eight counts of bribery between December 2018 and July 2019. Police have estimated that the former leader of Reform in Wales took at least £40,000 (US$53,000) in bribes.
Prosecutors said Gill, a member of the European Union legislature until the UK’s departure from the bloc in early 2020, was tasked by the former pro-Russia Ukrainian politician Oleg Voloshyn to make pro-Russian statements about events in Ukraine in the European Parliament and in opinion pieces to news outlets such as 112 Ukraine.

Russia launched its full-scale invasion of Ukraine in February 2022, having annexed parts of the country, including the Crimea peninsula, eight years earlier.
Sentencing him at London’s Central Criminal Court, better known as the Old Bailey, Justice Bobbie Cheema-Grubb said Gill had allowed money to “corrupt his moral compass” when it had been his duty as an elected official to “speak with honesty and conviction”.
“When you say what someone has paid you to say, you are not speaking with sincerity,” the judge said.
Gill was stopped at Manchester Airport in September 2021 as he attempted to travel to Russia, and was charged in February of this year following an investigation.
His mobile phone was examined and messages were found between him and Voloshyn, 44, that provided coded references to their financial dealings.
There also were references to other members of the European Parliament, including British representatives and politicians from the Netherlands.


“The communications between the two men showed that an established relationship existed between them,” prosecutor Mark Heywood said.
Gill was first elected to the European Parliament in 2014 for the anti-EU UK Independence Party, which was then led by Farage.
He later represented the Brexit Party, the next party led by Farage, and remained in the legislature until Britain left the EU in 2020.
He led the Welsh branch of the Brexit Party’s successor Reform UK during the 2021 elections in Wales but is no longer a member of the party, which is leading in Britain in opinion polls.
In a statement, Reform said Gill’s actions were “reprehensible, treasonous and unforgivable” and that it was pleased that “justice has been served”.
Voloshyn, who had links to media outlets such as 112 Ukraine, is believed to be living in Russia and is wanted by authorities in Britain and Ukraine.

data.title: Hong Kong’s BN(O) holders face tougher English test, possible income threshold
data.description:
Hongkongers hoping to settle in the United Kingdom under a bespoke visa pathway will face a tougher English test and potentially an income threshold as part of an overhaul of the country’s immigration system, British authorities have told the Post.
The confirmation came as BN (O) visa holders, who were relieved about the unchanged five-year settlement qualifying period revealed by the UK government on Thursday, plunged into anxiety hours later after reading the fine print of the consultation paper.
The planned overhaul of the country’s wider settlement system, unveiled by the Labour government on Thursday, will undergo a 12-week consultation, with British Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood saying that the changes would be adopted from April next year.
Hongkongers under the BN(O) scheme are among those exempted from plans to raise the qualifying period for settlement to 10 years.
But the British Home Office confirmed to the Post on Friday that those applying to settle in the country under the scheme would be subject to a tighter English language requirement, adding that an income threshold was being considered under the reform.
“Those on the BN(O) route must also meet the annual earnings contributions laid out in our model, but this will be subject to consultation,” a spokesman said.
Under the proposal, BN(O) settlement applicants must earn at least £12,570 (US$16,430), or about HK$128,000, annually for three to five years or have an “alternative amount of income”, which has not yet been specified.
BN(O) holders will also be required to meet the B2 English proficiency level of the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages, an “upper intermediate level” equivalent to the British A-level standard.
B2 level speakers are expected to understand the main ideas of complex texts on concrete or abstract topics, including some technical discussions.
“But our expectation is that the vast majority of BN(O)s are already at that level,” the spokesman said.
Currently, there are no English requirements to apply for a BN(O) visa, but candidates must meet the level B1 when applying to settle in the UK or for citizenship.
B1 is equivalent to a mid-range GCSE grade and is the “intermediate” level of the framework, with speakers able to understand and produce text on familiar topics and give opinions and descriptions.
Applicants should also have no criminal convictions, not be currently subject to litigation, nor have any outstanding government debts such as unpaid fines, taxes or medical fees.
According to the consultation paper, the UK government is soliciting opinions on the length of time the income requirement should apply and any grounds for exemptions.
“We also emphasise that the home secretary … in the [British parliament] expressed her continued commitment to providing clarity to Hong Kong visa holders as this consultation develops,” the spokesman said.
The shake-up has sparked concerns among Hongkongers on the BN(O) pathway about being unable to meet the tightened requirements.
“Many parents moved to Britain, and some of them are full-time parents who support their living with their savings or assets instead of working,” an anonymous user wrote on a social media page for Hongkongers in the UK.
“They could not meet the minimum annual earnings threshold.”
The comment was echoed by many others, with some saying that the job market in Britain was challenging, making it difficult to meet the requirements even if they were eager to take on part-time work.
Zoe Lee, a 60-year-old homemaker who moved to Britain with her family in April 2022, said she was extremely worried about the changing language requirement.
“I am so panicked that I will try to work hard to improve my English, including studying diligently and watching more news,” she told the Post.
She added that if she were unable to pass the test in the future, she would wait until she turned 65, at which point she would be exempt from the requirement under the current rules.
But the consultation paper did not say if the exemption would still apply.
Horace Wong, who moved to Britain in 2023 and works at a due diligence agency, said the new measures had a limited impact on the younger generation as many were proficient in English and had full-time jobs.
“But there are concerns for the older generation. I know some only have limited English proficiency, and they simply brought their life savings to the UK and retired,” the 27-year-old said.
Immigration consultant Benny Cheung Ka-hei said that some middle-aged or elderly Hongkongers moving to the UK faced challenges in securing jobs, but he expected more of them would meet the earnings threshold if assets and non-work income were counted.
Krish Kandiah, chairman of the Welcoming Committee for Hongkongers, a non-profit group, said that some of the moves were controversial and stressed that the proposed reforms would be subject to modifications, encouraging Hongkongers not to worry.
“There is a consultation period; it will be debated in our parliament,” he said.
“Often, some of the more eye-catching or extreme elements of a bill will get written out and end up with a more reasonable policy.”
In the policy paper, the British Home Office also shared an estimation that about 195,000 BN(O) visa holders would settle in the UK in the next five years.
It forecast a spike of 92,000 settlement applications in 2026, when the first batch of BN(O) visa holders would be eligible to apply for permanent residency under the five-year pathway.
The figure was forecast to shrink continuously afterwards, with only 7,000 BN(O) visa holders expected to file applications in 2030.
Britain launched the BN(O) migration pathway in January 2021 in response to the Beijing-imposed national security law, approving nearly 225,000 such visas for Hongkongers and recording more than 160,000 arrivals as of September this year.

data.title: 2 children among dead as Bangladesh rattled by magnitude 5.7 earthquake
data.description:
At least seven people, including two children, were killed and around 100 injured when a magnitude 5.7 earthquake struck Bangladesh on Friday, the government said, with buildings damaged in many areas including the densely populated capital Dhaka.
Tremors were felt in eastern states in neighbouring India that border Bangladesh, but there were no immediate reports of major damage there, authorities said.
Muhammad Yunus, the head of Bangladesh’s interim government, said the injured included students from Dhaka University, factory workers in the city of Gazipur and residents of Narsingdi, the epicentre of the earthquake.
Dhaka-based DBC Television said two children were among the dead. The fatalities occurred mostly from collapsed roofs and walls.

Earlier, police said three people had been killed when the railing of a six-storey building collapsed during the earthquake.
The three were shopping at a butcher’s shop in Armanitola, an area of Dhaka’s old city, and were taken to a hospital where they were pronounced dead, Prothom Alo newspaper said.
The Fire Service and Civil Defence department deployed firefighters to locations where people reported tilting buildings, as well as to a fire in Dhaka’s Baridhara neighbourhood.
Television stations broadcast footage of worried and crying relatives as the victims were brought to medical facilities, including at the state-run Dhaka Medical College Hospital. Dhaka University students jumped from the upper floors of their dorms during the quake.
Several of the injured taken to hospital from Narsingdi, about 40km (25 miles) east of Dhaka, were in a critical condition, health adviser Nurjahan Begum told reporters.
‘Everyone was terrified’
Dhaka residents rushed out of their homes as buildings shook and some makeshift structures collapsed, witnesses said.
“We felt a strong jolt and buildings were shaking like trees,” said resident Suman Rahman. “Staircases were jammed as people rushed down. Everyone was terrified, children were crying.”
The fire department said some people had been injured when bricks and loose cement fell from buildings that were under construction.

“I have never felt such a tremor in my life. We were at the office when the furniture started shaking. We rushed down the stairs to the street and saw other people on the road already,” said Sadman Sakib, who works in Dhaka.
The government was closely monitoring the situation and had directed departments to intensify rescue operations in affected areas, Yunus’ office said in the statement.
According to the USGS, though northern and southeastern Bangladesh are seismically active regions because of interactions between the India and Eurasia plates, the central region of the country is less active.
The USGS says that since 1950, only 14 earthquakes – with a magnitude of 5.5 and including two magnitude 6 quakes – have occurred within a 250km (156 mile) radius of Friday’s earthquake.
Experts warn that Bangladesh, a country of 170 million people, is less prepared for higher-magnitude quakes that could cause a lot of destruction.
Additional reporting by Associated Press

data.title: Meet Hong Kong’s history-making medal winners from the National Games
data.description:
Hong Kong’s athletes have had a historically good National Games, winning more golds, nine, and more medals (19) across more sports (six) than ever before.
The first two golds came before the opening ceremony and there was success on an almost daily basis after that, be it breaking long-standing city records or adding titles in events and sports for the first time.
From sailor Nicholas Halliday, who got things rolling on the sea, to the men’s foil team who grabbed a first fencing gold on Wednesday, this is our tribute to the city’s sporting heroes.
Gold
Nicholas Halliday
Sailing – men’s ILCA 7 (October 29)
Nicholas Halliday got the city’s historic gold rush up and running before we had even entered November.
Competing in the coastal city of Shanwei, the Olympian sailor won the protracted competition by achieving a leading net score of 33 points.

The event requires consistency across multiple races, with a sailor’s worst finish being discarded. Halliday’s strategic racing saw him finish fourth in the 11th and final round – more than enough to hold off Shanxi’s Tang Yuanshuai by a decisive seven-point margin.
It also made Halliday the first sailing gold medallist from Hong Kong in any edition of the national multi-sport showpiece.
Ceci Lee Sze-wing
Cycling – women’s road race (November 9); women’s Omnium (November 17)
Ceci Lee Sze-wing crowned herself the undisputed queen of Hong Kong cycling, winning three gold medals, an unprecedented achievement for a city athlete at a single Games.
Her first came in the hours before the opening ceremony, when she successfully defended the women’s road race title she won in Shaanxi in 2021, launching a thrilling and decisive sprint on the home straight of the 138.7km Zhuhai course.
While her teammates returned to track cycling action at the Hong Kong Velodrome days later, Lee took a rest, returning to a golden weekend, which she wrapped up with Omnium gold in front of a packed crowd.

Ceci Lee and Chloe Leung Wing-yee
Cycling – women’s Madison (November 16)
In between winning her individual titles, Lee raced around the track in the women’s Madison alongside Chloe Leung.
The pair produced a composed performance, blowing away the opposition along with Leung’s pre-race nerves, and she said it was Lee’s new-found bullishness that had set the tone.
“Before the competition today, Ceci told me: ‘The road race was so difficult and still I managed it. We’ll be OK with this’,” Leung said. “And I thought to myself, if she was so confident in me, there was no reason for me to be under-confident.”
Siobhan Haughey
Swimming – women’s 200m freestyle (November 13); women’s 100m freestyle (November 15)
The city’s star of the pool proved she is one of Hong Kong’s greatest athletes ever by bringing home two golds in Shenzhen, a performance that came after an extended break at the start of the year and a back injury.
She dominated her favourite 200m freestyle event first with a time of one minute, 54.85 seconds, and 48 hours later touched the wall in 52.89 seconds to take the women’s 100m freestyle final.
Already Hong Kong’s most decorated Olympian, Haughey became the city’s first athlete to win two gold medals at a single China Games.

Ian Ho Yentou
Swimming – men’s 50m Freestyle (November 16)
The 28-year-old produced a scintillating performance to take gold in the men’s 50m freestyle in Shenzhen, consigning China’s Olympic star Pan Zhanle to the role of also-ran.
Ho’s success marked the first time a Hong Kong male swimmer has won a gold medal at the multi-sports games in China.
And what was just as impressive, the veteran swimmer won in a time of 21.71 seconds, shattering the city’s record, which he set only months ago at the World Aquatics Championships in Singapore.
Rugby Sevens
Men’s team event (November 14)
Hong Kong men’s rugby sevens team did not let their home crowd down at Kai Tak Stadium, beating Shandong 26-19 in a rip-roaring final.
The city team were given a mighty scare when Shandong recovered from 12-0 down to lead 14-12 with only four minutes remaining.
But Julien Bourron answered the call to be the team’s hero, barrelling through fierce resistance to score under the posts before gathering himself to kick the conversion.
Cado Lee Ka-to pounced on some clumsy Shandong play to put Hong Kong out of sight and reduce Liu Luda’s late reply to a mere consolation.

Fencing
Men’s foil team (November 19)
Hong Kong’s men won the city’s first National Games gold in fencing, beating Fujian 45-34 in the final of the foil team event at Kai Tak Arena.
Seeded third, the home quartet eliminated Zhejiang and Jiangsu en route to the highly anticipated bout against the top seeds and defending champions.
The team included the city’s top fencers Ryan Choi Chun-yin and Cheung Ka-long, along with Leung Chin-yu and Lawrence Ng Lok-wang, and it was Ng’s performance in a nervy quarter-final against Zhejiang that drew praise from head coach Greg Koenig.
“Leung was not there in the first match but Lawrence came on and saved us,” he said.
Silver
Aaron Ho Sze-long
Fencing – men’s individual sabre (November 15)
Aaron Ho Sze-long brought home a historic first sabre medal for the city in claiming the men’s silver at Kai Tak Arena.
After emerging from the group stage, Ho grew in confidence and swept aside successive opponents, including 2018 Asian Games bronze medallist Xu Yingming of Guangdong, to reach the final – something no sabreist from Hong Kong had managed before.
However, a 15-7 loss at the hands of Shandong representative Wei Zhenhao marked the end of the amazing run by Ho, who applauded the thousands of fans after the heartbreaking defeat.

Triathlon
Mixed Relay team event (November 16)
Hong Kong’s triathletes sealed team silver following a roller-coaster race, finishing just 11 seconds behind Shandong, who finished in one hour, 24 minutes and five seconds.
Cade Wright led off with a blistering opening leg. Collapsing in a heap as she finished, Wright handed over to Robin Elg in the lead. Elg then kept the lead and delivered the team’s quickest leg at 19 minutes and 47 seconds.
Bailee Brown took over from Elg with Hong Kong in pole position, but retained it only until the closing run, rivalling several provinces’ best athletes.
When she handed over to Oscar Coggins on the final leg, Hong Kong were third. Coggins then made up ground and launched an exhilarating attack on the leading pair’s blindside during the cycling. But Shandong’s Zhang Xirui exploited his strong run to snatch gold.
Bronze
Tennis
Youth men’s team (November 12)
The bronze won by the youth men’s team marked the city’s first podium in a tennis team event at the competition.
As the tournament features no bronze medal match, the team – Jack Cheng, Nicholas Cheng and James Wong – secured their place on the podium after a hard-fought semi-final against top seeds Jiangsu at Hengqin International Tennis Centre in Zhuhai that they ultimately lost 2-0.
Oscar Coggins
Triathlon – men’s individual race (November 15)
Triathlete Coggins claimed the first major Games medal of his career, finishing in 1:49.21 behind Henan’s Fan Junjie and Chen Qi of Liaoning.
Adam Mak Sai-ting
Swimming – men’s 200m breaststroke (November 15)
Adam Mak Sai-ting became the first Hongkonger to ever win a men’s swimming medal at the Games, taking bronze in a city record of two minutes, 9.93 seconds.

Kaylin Hsieh Sin-yan
Fencing – women’s individual épée (November 15)
Kaylin Hsieh Sin-yan won a bronze medal after overcoming Mo Wanlin of Guangdong 15-9 in the third-place play-off. Unbeaten through the group stage and picking up wins in the early knockout rounds, Hsieh lost 14-8 in the semi-finals to former world champion Lin Sheng, who pocketed the event’s gold later.
Cheung Ka-long
Fencing – men’s individual foil (November 16)
After losing to Fujian’s Xu Jie in the semi-finals, Cheung Ka-long stormed to bronze with an emphatic 15-2 victory over Zeng Zhaoran.
“I am not happy with just a bronze medal,” he said. “I told myself I could not lose [before the semi-final] because I didn’t want to fight for the bronze as it was stressful. But Xu was really good and I didn’t grasp my opportunities.”
Siobhan Haughey
Swimming – women’s 50m breaststroke; women’s 50m freestyle (November 17)
Two finals in five minutes, two bronze medals, Siobhan Haughey added to her growing legend and signed off from the National Games in fine style.
Next up, the Asian Games.
“There is no time for celebration, but I am very happy and satisfied with my performance at the National Games,” Haughey said afterwards. “I now understand where the standards are and know there is still plenty of room for improvement.”
Coleman Wong Chak-lam
Tennis – men’s singles (November 19)
Coleman Wong Chak-lam’s impressive run was stopped in the quarter-finals by Wu Yibing, who won 6-7, 7-6, 6-4 in a three-set epic.
Still, Wong’s medal was the city’s first in tennis singles, a feat he called a “milestone achievement”.

data.title: China’s JL-1 missile beats US rivals, but will launch platform hold it back?
data.description:
China’s Jinglei-1 (JL-1) missile could pose a nuclear threat to Alaska – and potentially the rest of the United States – though it still has significant limitations, according to a Chinese military magazine.
The JL-1, which made its debut at China’s Victory Day parade in September alongside other strategic missiles, is a nuclear-capable air-launched ballistic missile. It can be carried by the Chinese air force’s H-6N strategic bombers and is believed to be the final jigsaw piece needed to complete the People’s Liberation Army’s nuclear triad, or the ability to deliver nuclear weapons from platforms in the air, land and sea.
It is the world’s only long-range hypersonic missile capable of carrying nuclear warheads, putting China ahead of the United States and Russia in this emerging weapons category, according to an analysis in this month’s issue of Ordnance Industry Science Technology magazine.
The JL-1, whose name means “Shocking Thunder-1”, reportedly has a range of up to 8,000km (4,971 miles), while an H-6N bomber has a combat range of up to 4,000km if supported by aerial refuelling.
According to the analysis, this combination gives the weapon system an effective operational reach of over 8,000km under very conservative estimates, enough to cover targets such as Alaska or locations within the third island chain in the Pacific.
“Since Alaska is the core base of the US national missile defence system, the JL-1 would have no real combat purpose if it cannot pose a credible threat to it,” the article said.


If a Chinese bomber took off from an airbase in Russia’s far east – a possibility demonstrated during a joint air exercise in July last year – it could potentially deliver nuclear warheads to much of the continental US, the report said.
It compared the Chinese missile to Russian and American air-launched nuclear-capable missiles.
The US Air Force’s only nuclear-armed cruise missile in service is the AGM-86B, a 43-year-old system with a 2,400km range, subsonic speed and outdated inertial and terrain guidance “ill suited for modern deterrence requirements”, according to the article.
Its planned successor, the AGM-181A, which is expected to enter service around 2030, will have enhanced stealth and guidance but will still travel at subsonic speeds, putting it “one generation behind the JL-1”, according to the report.
The US Air Force’s AGM-183A hypersonic nuclear-capable missile programme has faced significant setbacks, and the funding was cancelled in 2024.
Russia’s Kh-102 and Kh-BD long-range nuclear-capable cruise missiles, while advanced in terms of range and precision, are also subsonic. The country’s only air-launched hypersonic missile is the Kinzhal (Kh-47M2), which can carry a nuclear warhead but has a relatively smaller range of 1,500km to 2,000km.
According to the analysis, no current US or Russian missile matches the strategic reach and technological edge of China’s JL-1.


However, the JL-1’s launch platform is its ultimate limitation. The 15-metre (49-foot) missile is too long to fit into any aircraft’s internal weapons bay, including that of the H-20 stealth bomber now under development, making it “exclusively designed for the H-6N”, it said.
The H-6 series is based on the Soviet Tupolev Tu-16, known to Nato as the Badger, which went into service in 1954. The H-6N, publicly revealed in 2019, is equipped with an aerial refuelling probe, significantly extending its operational range.
But it suffers from limitations inherent to its legacy airframe, including moderate payload capacity, subsonic performance and lack of stealth, and it cannot match Western bombers such as the B-2 Spirit or B-21 Raider.
“China needs both high-speed and stealthy penetration when it comes to a strategic strike,” the analysis said. “The H-6N, as an interim solution, is far from sufficient.”
Since at least 2016, China has been working on its H-20 next-generation long-range stealth bomber, though there have yet to be any confirmed images or details about the aircraft.
The latest high-level official mention of it was in March last year, when PLA Air Force deputy commander Wang Wei said the bomber would be revealed “soon”, adding that its development had “no bottlenecks”.
US defence assessments suggest the H-20 might not debut until the 2030s.
The magazine article described the progress of the H-20 as “footsteps on the stairs are heard, but nobody comes down”, suggesting that while development was under way, no milestones could be seen. It warned that if the deployment of the H-20 lagged too far behind that of the B-21, China could face a prolonged period of strategic disadvantage and military vulnerability.
It noted that the successful development of the JL-1 had bought time for the H-20 programme, potentially allowing for, or even leading to, further delays.
“With the H-6N and JL-1 carrying out strategic deterrence patrols, the H-20 can be developed at a less pressured pace for better design refinements, correction of potential flaws and thorough testing,” it said.
“The H-20 will not merely be a stealthy strategic bomber; it could potentially evolve into a family of stealth platforms, including a strategic command aircraft, airborne early warning platform, electronic warfare aircraft, transport, or even aerial refuelling tanker.”

data.title: Hong Kong taxi driver gets 2 years’ jail over collision that killed cyclist, 17
data.description:
A Hong Kong judge has called on elderly drivers to regularly assess their ability to continue with their jobs, as she jailed a 70-year-old cabby for two years over a fatal collision that killed a teenage cyclist last year.
Lau Chor-hi was sentenced on Friday for causing the death of student Wong Ho-chun, 17, after speeding along a bridge section of Kwai Tsing Road in the early hours of July 3, 2024.
West Kowloon Court heard that the defendant’s taxi was moving at 85 to 103 km/h when it crashed into Wong’s bicycle at 1.49am on the fast lane of the Tsing Yi-bound carriageway, exceeding the legal limit of 70km/h for the section.
Wong suffered extensive injuries to his head and body and never regained consciousness before he was pronounced dead in hospital at 9pm that day.
Dashcam footage showed the taxi driver, who was on night duty, had a clear view of the surroundings but failed to hit the brakes or make any attempt to avoid the student before the collision.
Lau pleaded guilty to dangerous driving causing death last month.
The court heard Lau had been reluctant to retire before the accident, as he had enjoyed chatting and having afternoon tea with other drivers and did not want to burden his three children.
Lau had decided that he would stop driving when he reached 70 in April, following his children’s repeated advice.
The defence said Lau had suffered severe depression and required psychiatric treatment, as he constantly reproached himself for his mistake that cost a teenager his life.
Deputy District Judge Ivy Chui Yee-mei took the accused to task for his “irresponsible, selfish and reckless” driving manner, saying he could not have failed to notice the cyclist as his passenger could see him from the back seat and even warned him of the danger.
Chui said the defendant, who had only been fined once for going through a red light in his 40-year career, should have driven more responsibly given his experience.
“A professional driver, be it a bus driver, a taxi driver, a minibus driver or a truck driver, should always remember their duty to drive safely while making a living from it,” she said.
“If drivers are unable to maintain the requisite standards of driving, such as by reason of their age, motor vehicles can become lethal and pose dangers to the public.”
Chui reduced Lau’s three-year sentence by a third to reflect his timely plea.
The court also disqualified Lau from driving for five years and ordered him to undertake a driving improvement course at his own expense.
Dangerous driving causing death is punishable by up to 10 years' imprisonment.
The Court of Appeal’s sentencing guidelines stipulate that an offender is sentenced to no more than three years behind bars if the case only involves a momentary dangerous error of judgment or the driver’s irresponsible or habitually unacceptable conduct.
Hong Kong does not ban elderly people from driving taxis, but those aged 70 and above are required to prove they are physically fit before they can renew their licences for up to three years at a time.
Transport Department figures show the number of elderly taxi drivers involved in accidents has jumped by more than 40 per cent since 2022.
The government proposed tightening physical fitness requirements for commercial vehicle drivers after an 80-year-old cabby crashed his vehicle into a hotel taxi rank and killed a Filipino tourist earlier this year.

data.title: US ‘biggest recipient’ of China debt since 2000; Beijing’s warning: SCMP daily highlights
data.description:
Catch up on some of SCMP’s biggest China stories of the day. If you would like to see more of our reporting, please consider subscribing.
1. China has lent more to US than any other country since 2000, report finds
China’s lending since the turn of the century has been “vastly” larger than previously understood, with loans and grants increasingly going to developed countries including the United States – the largest recipient – according to a new report by an American university research team.
2. Is Beijing’s spat with Japan sending a message to other countries over Taiwan?
Beijing’s recent fury directed towards Japan is hammering home a point on Taiwan to the wider world, especially the United States and its allies, analysts in mainland China have suggested.
3. ‘Unpredictable risks’: China to see record graduates next year amid jobs crunch

China is preparing for an unprecedented wave of new university graduates next summer – a record 12.7 million outgoing students – intensifying pressure on an already strained job market and a slowing economy.
4. US boycott and China-Japan rift loom large over South Africa G20 summit
Leaders from the world’s wealthiest and largest economies will gather in Johannesburg for the Group of 20 summit this weekend, as geopolitical friction threatens to derail the event.
5. Japan’s export of lethal weapons to US ‘extremely dangerous’: Chinese analysts

Japan’s export of lethal weapons sent an “extremely dangerous signal” indicating expanded military capabilities, Chinese observers and media said on Thursday as relations between the two countries continued to nosedive.
6. China’s EV parts could be in Tesla’s crosshairs – is a total ‘de-risk’ feasible?
Tesla’s reported plan to stop using Chinese-made components in US-built cars aligns with a “significant strategy” in national industrial policy, with some American giants seeking to “completely de-risk” supply chains amid an increasingly tense geopolitical climate, an automotive executive said.
7. Beijing launches historic preservation campaign to cement South China Sea claims

China has launched a campaign to preserve historical sites on the disputed Woody Island, or Yongxing Island in Chinese, as Beijing looks to cement its territorial claims in the South China Sea.
